panels.description: Request success rate. Normal (green) if 99.5% or higher; Critical (red) if below 98%. Only normal completions where `finished_reason=“stop”` are counted as successes.
panels.title: Success Rate

panels.description: Number of requests currently being processed (Running) + on hold (Waiting). A sharp increase indicates a traffic surge.
panels.title: Active Requests

panels.description: Scheduler efficiency. Running/(Running+Waiting). A value of 100% indicates that all requests are processed immediately. A value below 70% indicates overload.
panels.title: System Efficiency

panels.description: The time taken to generate the first token (P99). A key indicator of the initial response speed experienced by users. A time of less than one second is ideal.
panels.title: TTFT P99

panels.description: [Critical] Preemption rate. Requests currently in progress have been terminated due to insufficient KV cache. Immediate investigation is required if this exceeds 0. Consider increasing GPU memory or reducing batch size.
panels.title: Preemption Rate

panels.description: The total time taken from request receipt to response completion. P50 indicates that half of the requests are completed within this time, whilst P99 indicates that 99% of requests are completed within this time. If P99 rises sharply, it is necessary to analyse potential bottlenecks.
panels.title: E2E Request Latency

panels.description: The proportion of latency between Prefill (input processing) and Decode (output generation). If Prefill is high, long input prompts become a bottleneck; if Decode is high, long output generation becomes a bottleneck.
panels.title: Inference Stage Breakdown

panels.description: Comparison of TTFT (Time to First Token) and TPOT (Time per Token). A high TTFT indicates a prefill bottleneck, whilst a high TPOT indicates a decode bottleneck.
panels.title: TTFT vs TPOT

panels.description: Number of tokens processed per second. Input (Prompt) = input tokens; Output (Generation) = output tokens. Higher throughput indicates greater system efficiency.
panels.title: Token Throughput

panels.description: The ratio of output tokens to input tokens. A high ratio indicates long input + short output (RAG), whilst a low ratio indicates short input + long output (generation/summarisation).
panels.title: Token I/O Ratio

panels.description: Percentage of operations saved by the prefix cache. The higher the value, the more operations the cache skips via prefilling, thereby conserving GPU resources. (1 – Actual operations / Total inputs) * 100
panels.title: Prefix Cache Savings

panels.description: Distribution of prompt token counts by request. The darker the colour, the more requests of that length there are. If there is a concentration in a specific range, optimisation for that length should be considered.
panels.title: Request Length Heatmap (Prompt)

panels.description: The time a request has spent waiting in the queue. Latency increases when the GPU is busy. If this remains consistently high, consider scaling out the instance.
panels.title: Request Queue Time

panels.description: GPU KV Cache utilisation. A memory area used to store intermediate calculation results. Exercise caution if utilisation exceeds 80%; there is a risk of preemption if it exceeds 95%.
panels.title: GPU KV Cache Usage

panels.description: Prefix cache hit rate. The proportion of identical system prompts or few-shot examples that are reused. A rate of 70% or higher is considered efficient; this is a key metric in RAG environments.
panels.title: Prefix Cache Hit Rate

panels.description: Number of requests by scheduler state. Running = Inference in progress on the GPU; Waiting = In the queue; Swapped = Swapped to memory. If the number of requests in the Waiting state continues to rise, the system is overloaded.
panels.title: Scheduler State

panels.description: The frequency of Python GC events and the process’s memory usage. If GC occurs frequently or RSS continues to increase, there may be a memory leak.
panels.title: Python GC & Memory

panels.description: Distribution of request completion reasons. stop = normal termination (EOS), length = maximum length reached, abort = termination. A high abort rate indicates a problem.
panels.title: Finish Reason Distribution

panels.description: Total request throughput vs. successful requests. If there is a gap, it indicates the presence of failed requests. They should remain consistent to be considered normal.
panels.title: Throughput vs Success

panels.description: CPU utilization
panels.title: CPU Usage
